title: Calibrating Monetary Policy - San Francisco Fed
gist: The SF Fed unveils a Policy Calibration Tool (PCT) built on Barnichon-Mesters (2023) methodology that maps a policymaker's economic outlook and dual-mandate preferences into a preferred fed funds rate path; applied to 2025-26 tariff shocks, the tool prescribes sharply raising rates if tariffs are read as a demand/cost-push inflation shock, but cutting rates if tariffs are read as an uncertainty-driven weak-demand shock — meaning the 'correct' Fed response to tariffs is genuinely ambiguous and hinges entirely on which transmission channel dominates.

insights.claim: The SF Fed built a new Policy Calibration Tool (PCT), based on Barnichon and Mesters (2023), that computes a preferred federal funds rate path from just two subjective inputs (a status-quo economic outlook and a chosen weight between inflation and employment goals) plus one objective input (estimated policy multipliers), without needing a full structural economic model.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: This is a lightweight, model-agnostic tool investors and Fed-watchers could use to reverse-engineer what rate path a given FOMC member's stated economic views imply, improving the ability to forecast dissent and policy surprises.
insights.quote: The PCT computes the federal funds rate path that will best achieve the dual mandate given two inputs.
insights.locator: Recent research by Barnichon and Mesters (2023) introduced a new methodology

insights.claim: The PCT works by comparing a baseline (status-quo) macro forecast against alternative policy-scenario forecasts generated using estimated policy multipliers, letting a policymaker search across counterfactual rate paths for the one that best matches their preferred inflation/employment tradeoff.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Analysts can use this same counterfactual logic to stress-test how the Fed might respond to any given shock (tariffs, energy prices, financial stress) once they estimate the multipliers and the shock's demand/supply split.
insights.locator: Barnichon and Mesters (2023) propose an approach that does not require

insights.claim: Monetary policy inherently forces a tradeoff between price stability and maximum employment, because the Fed generally cannot fully counter an adverse supply shock's inflationary effect without imposing some cost on employment.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: This tradeoff framing is the analytical backbone for interpreting any future supply-shock (tariffs, oil, supply chains) as a forced choice, not a free lunch, for the FOMC.
insights.quote: monetary policy cannot generally counter the inflationary effects of an adverse supply shock without some cost to employment
insights.locator: In particular, monetary policymakers sometimes need to balance a tradeoff

themes.theme: Estimated policy multipliers and their limits

insights.claim: Using 1990-2007 U.S. data (chosen to be stable and to avoid the post-2008 zero lower bound period), Barnichon and Mesters estimate that a 25-basis-point federal funds rate shock sustained over a year takes about a year to substantially affect inflation.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Confirms long, variable lags in monetary transmission — investors should not expect current hikes/cuts to show up in inflation prints for roughly a year, supporting patience in front-running Fed pivots.
insights.quote: monetary policy may take about a year to substantially affect inflation
insights.locator: The estimated policy multipliers, shown by solid lines in Figure 1

insights.claim: The estimated multipliers imply that a federal funds rate shock large enough to lower inflation by 0.1 percentage point after two years would raise unemployment by about 0.3 percentage point over the same period, quantifying a roughly 3-to-1 unemployment cost per unit of disinflation.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete rule-of-thumb ratio for how much labor-market pain the Fed is implicitly willing to accept per point of disinflation, useful for gauging how aggressive future tightening cycles might get before the Fed backs off.
insights.quote: a federal funds rate shock large enough to bring inflation down about 0.1 percentage point after two years would be expected to increase unemployment about 0.3 percentage point over the same period
insights.locator: Second, a federal funds rate shock large enough to bring inflation down

insights.claim: The multiplier estimates carry wide 95% confidence bands and the PCT assumes the economy responds linearly to shocks (treating small and large recessions the same way), meaning the tool's prescriptions should be treated as indicative rather than precise.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Investors should discount the PCT's specific rate-path numbers and instead use it directionally; large, nonlinear shocks (e.g., a severe recession) could make the tool's guidance materially wrong.
insights.quote: The tool also uses an assumption that the economy behaves linearly
insights.locator: We caution that the PCT should be seen only as an indicative tool

themes.theme: Tariffs as an ambiguous shock: two scenarios, two opposite rate calls

insights.claim: In the SF Fed's illustrative cost-push tariff scenario, inflation rises to near 6% (from the professional forecaster baseline) before slowly reverting to the Fed's 2% goal, and unemployment rises to about 6% in tandem, reflecting tariffs acting as a supply-side cost shock.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Marks the upper bound of a stagflationary tariff scenario the Fed is explicitly modeling; a 6% CPI print alongside 6% unemployment would be a genuine stagflation shock investors should have tail-risk hedges for.
insights.quote: the scenario assumes that inflation rises substantially, close to 6%
insights.locator: In the first scenario, the tariff increase has a cost-push effect

insights.claim: Under this cost-push tariff scenario, the PCT prescribes sharply raising the federal funds rate above baseline in the first year, then gradually returning toward the baseline path, accepting higher unemployment as the cost of fighting tariff-driven inflation.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: If incoming data shows tariffs feeding through primarily as cost-push inflation, investors should expect a more hawkish Fed path than currently priced by market-implied forward rates.
insights.quote: the PCT would call for raising the interest rate to fight off inflation, even though this comes at the cost of higher unemployment
insights.locator: Under the cost-push scenario (triangle line), the PCT would call for

insights.claim: In the SF Fed's alternative weak-demand tariff scenario, tariff-induced policy uncertainty depresses consumer and investor confidence, pushing unemployment up (similarly to the cost-push case) but pushing inflation down, falling below 1.5% by end-2026 rather than up toward 6%.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: This is the disinflationary/recessionary tail case for tariffs — if inflation surprises to the downside alongside rising unemployment, that is a strong signal the market and Fed should be repricing toward rate cuts, not hikes.
insights.quote: inflation dips below the baseline forecast, falling below 1.5% by the end of 2026
insights.locator: The second scenario is motivated by recent research that finds

insights.claim: Under the weak-demand tariff scenario, the PCT prescribes cutting the federal funds rate rather than raising it, since a more expansionary stance would help both inflation and unemployment revert faster to target/natural-rate levels.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: The exact same tariff shock — same headline event — justifies rate cuts instead of hikes depending on the demand/supply read; investors should track uncertainty/confidence indicators (not just CPI) to determine which regime is unfolding.
insights.quote: the PCT would call for the policy path to go in the opposite direction: If uncertainty stemming from tariff or other policy changes were to lead to lower inflation, the PCT would call for cutting the interest rate
insights.locator: Under the weak demand scenario (circle line), the PCT would call for

insights.claim: Because the correct Fed response to the 2025-26 tariff increases depends entirely on whether one views them as primarily a demand shock (via uncertainty) or a supply shock (via cost-push), disagreement among individual FOMC policymakers about the right rate path could be substantial.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Investors should expect wider-than-usual dispersion in FOMC dot plots and more contentious policy votes as long as the tariff transmission channel remains unresolved — a source of rate-path volatility distinct from the data itself.
insights.quote: The uncertainty surrounding the effects of large tariff changes implies that uncertainty about the best course of action and thus disagreement among policymakers could also be substantial.

insights.claim: The tariff scenarios presented are explicitly illustrative constructs and not a quantitative assessment of the actual 2025 tariffs' effects, so they should be read as bounding cases rather than the Fed's actual forecast.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Investors should not mistake this letter for an official SF Fed tariff forecast; it is a methodology demonstration, so the 6% inflation and sub-1.5% inflation figures are illustrative bounds, not point predictions to trade on directly.
insights.quote: they are illustrative and do not reflect any specific quantitative assessment of the actual effects of the 2025 tariffs
insights.locator: To simplify the analysis, we consider two hypothetical scenarios

themes.theme: Identifying policy shocks via natural experiments

insights.claim: Because economists cannot run controlled experiments on the macroeconomy, researchers rely on 'natural experiments' — Fed policy changes made for reasons unrelated to the short-run path of inflation and unemployment, such as responses to financial-market stress or shifts in FOMC membership — to isolate the causal effect of rate changes.
insights.speaker: Aayush Singh
insights.so_what: Explains the empirical basis for the multiplier estimates underlying the PCT; investors evaluating the tool's credibility should understand its causal identification rests on a fairly narrow set of historical 'clean' shocks, not the full history of Fed moves.
